entries.text: For relative-to-attribute questions, plan the shortest bridge: (subject -> resolve the relative -> fetch only the requested terminal attribute). First, perform a high-confidence ID step to ground the relative (use strict name/context matching and type constraints). Then query only the target attribute on that entity; do not open sibling attributes unless needed for disambiguation. Enable early stopping once a grounded, high-confidence hit for the target slot is found. At synthesis, select the terminal attribute value (not the bridge entity).
entries.rationale: High-scoring runs consistently resolved the bridge entity first, limited hops, avoided parallel sibling lookups, stopped early, and extracted the terminal attribute; the low-scoring run pursued redundant branches and meta checks that didn’t advance the asked slot.

entries.text: Enforce an answer-type guardrail and node filtering: derive the expected answer type from the question (e.g., place/date/person/number) and only accept spans from nodes whose schema/type matches that slot; exclude verification/meta nodes as answer sources. Prefer attribute values directly attached to the grounded bridge entity and supported by the same or tightly-linked evidence.
entries.rationale: Winners constrained extraction to the correct slot and avoided selecting from verification nodes; the losing run lacked this filter and explored redundant checks, increasing error risk.

entries.text: For easy yes/no comparisons, start with a single-hop, joint query that asks the comparison directly. Accept the result only if the snippet explicitly answers the boolean and is high-confidence (>=0.7) with clear grounding; otherwise fall back to two parallel lookups of the same attribute for each entity and compare. Synthesize only the final Yes/No with decisive citation(s) and use early stopping once a confident resolution is found to save tokens.
entries.rationale: Winning runs prioritized a direct, grounded single-hop check and only decomposed when confidence was low, yielding accurate answers with fewer tokens. This decision policy is broadly transferable to yes/no tasks.

entries.text: When backing off to parallel checks, enforce evidence hygiene and normalization: disambiguate entities (strict name/type/role), query the exact target attribute, reject related-but-different properties, and normalize values (units, spellings, aliases, temporal scope) before comparing. Require each sub-answer to meet the confidence threshold (>=0.7) and be grounded to the correct entity; output only the boolean, not intermediate attributes, and keep retrieval tight (add only lightweight extra lookups if confidence is borderline).
entries.rationale: Successful trajectories avoided attribute conflation and ensured each side of the comparison was independently grounded and normalized before synthesis, improving reliability on comparative yes/no questions.

entries.text: For venue-anchored jurisdiction/location questions, use a strict two-hop bridge: (1) resolve the venue/facility/campus to its host city, normalized as "City, State/Region, Country" with high-confidence grounding and type checks; (2) from that normalized city, fetch only the requested administrative unit (county/state/province, etc.). Gate hop 2 on a grounded hop 1, enforce an answer-type guard so only the administrative unit can be finalized (never the venue or the city), and cite an authoritative administrative source for the final span. If multiple plausible host cities remain, branch minimally; otherwise stop after these two hops.
entries.rationale: High-reward runs consistently gated the second hop on a confidently grounded city normalized with region and enforced answer-type selection of the administrative unit; the low-reward run lacked reliable two-hop execution/type guarding. This pattern generalizes to venue/facility→jurisdiction questions.

entries.text: Optimize retrieval for 2-hop bridges: issue one narrowly scoped lookup per hop, reuse the resolved city string/ID directly for hop 2, and constrain hop 2 to the known region to avoid cross-jurisdiction mismatches. Avoid redundant retries/parallel branches; only branch to resolve true ambiguity. Prefer a single authoritative administrative source for the final mapping and return only the terminal unit, not intermediates.
entries.rationale: Winning trajectories minimized tokens by reusing the grounded entity, scoping by region, and avoiding unnecessary branching while still providing precise citations. These efficiency controls are transferable across bridge_2hop tasks.

entries.text: Strict two-hop for attribute-of-creator/role-holder: (1) Resolve the correct person for the work/entity with high-confidence disambiguation and require an explicit tie-in (credits/filmography/official listing) that links the person to the work; (2) Bind hop 2 to that exact entity (same page/ID) and extract only the requested attribute from structured fields, normalizing aliases (e.g., nationality/citizenship/birthplace) and validating with an in-page corroboration. Gate hop 2 on a grounded hop 1; if the tie-in is weak or attributes conflict, re-query hop 1 with role/year/context qualifiers and retry once. Synthesize only the terminal attribute and cite evidence that jointly shows the work–person link and the attribute source. Keep retrieval narrowly scoped and avoid redundant branches/retries once both hops are grounded.
entries.rationale: Winning runs strictly entity-locked hop 2 to the person resolved in hop 1, required explicit linkage to the work to avoid namesake bleed, preferred structured attribute fields with a quick consistency check, and returned only the attribute with minimal retrieval. Losing runs drifted to similarly named people, used broad second-hop searches, or over-retried without improving grounding.

entries.text: Entity/type guardrails for 2-hop bridges: derive the expected answer type from the question and only accept a terminal span that matches it; return the target attribute, not the bridge entity. Carry forward key descriptors and regional cues from the prompt to constrain hop-1 disambiguation and require high-confidence grounding. Lock hop 2 to the resolved entity’s exact page/ID and query only the requested attribute. Prefer the resolved entity’s canonical page or authoritative profile; perform a lightweight cross-check and explicit type check to catch drift. If the hop-2 result fails the type constraint, auto-refine and retry minimal alternates for hop 1. When the bridge remains uncertain, try a cheap 1-hop direct query for the target attribute as a sanity check before deeper retries. Stop early once two grounded, consistent hops are achieved; avoid extra hops or parallel branches.
entries.rationale: High-scoring solutions combined strict entity binding and answer-type filtering with minimal, corroborated retrieval, whereas lower-scoring ones lacked type/ID locks, causing attribute drift and unnecessary retries.

entries.text: Bordering-county 2-hop for geography: (1) Disambiguate the seed place to a specific location with state/region/country qualifiers and expected_type=location; ground with high-confidence evidence (geo infobox/gazetteer). If homonyms exist, run shallow parallel checks on top candidates and prune to the best-supported one (>=0.7 confidence) before proceeding. (2) From the resolved containing county (lock to its page/ID), fetch its neighboring counties and enforce type=county and same-level jurisdiction; avoid cross-jurisdiction neighbors unless the prompt explicitly allows it. Handle multiplicity by using available context to pick a unique neighbor; if none, surface ambiguity rather than guessing. Synthesize only the neighboring county name (not the seed place or intermediate county). Keep retrieval tight to concise geo infoboxes/authoritative admin sources and stop after two grounded hops.
entries.rationale: High-scoring runs explicitly disambiguated the place with state/region constraints and expected_type=location, then bound hop 2 to the resolved county's neighbor list and returned only a neighbor county. Low-scoring runs skipped the bridge or mis-selected entities. This pattern generalizes to geography questions that require a bordering administrative unit via a two-hop bridge.

entries.text: Descriptor- and type-guarded 2-hop with direct-lookup fallback: Use a deliberate 2-hop bridge when the clue depends on an attribute of a related entity. Hop 1: resolve the related entity with strict name/context matching and descriptor-/role-based and regional constraints; require high-confidence grounding and prune to the best-supported candidate. Gate hop 2 on a grounded hop 1. Hop 2: fetch only the requested terminal attribute and enforce an answer-type guard (e.g., island/place/date/person); reject candidates that violate the type and auto-refine/retry minimal alternates. Lock hop 2 to the hop-1 entity/page and cross-check the final span across 1–2 sources. Efficiency: before or when hop 1 is uncertain, attempt a scoped 1-hop direct lookup of the target attribute to avoid error propagation; stop early once a high-confidence, type-matching span is found. Keep retrieval tight to the minimal supporting chunk and avoid unnecessary parallel branches; synthesize only the terminal attribute, not the bridge entity.
entries.rationale: Winners propagated descriptor/type constraints across hops, enforced target-type validation, and used minimal retrieval with early stopping; losers ignored descriptors and returned wrong types. Adding a general pattern with a direct 1-hop fallback prevents error propagation and saves tokens.

entries.text: Org-HQ city via related-entity 2-hop: (1) Resolve the related entity to the correct organization (e.g., network/channel/operator) with strict type/role checks; exclude production studios, local affiliates, similarly named brands, and regional arms unless the relation explicitly targets them. Lock to the org’s canonical page/ID or official corporate site. (2) From that exact entity, extract only the headquarters city. Enforce answer-type=city: reject countries/regions/campuses or ownership facts. If hop 2 returns an off-type or mixed location (metro/region lists), issue one targeted follow-up for the "Headquarters"/"Headquarters location (city)" field and select the city value. Cross-verify with the parent company’s official corporate listing/regulatory filings. Add a plausibility check (city-level, major metro consistency with known country/region) and re-query if the city seems implausibly small or mismatched. Efficiency: cap to one precise lookup per hop, allow at most one targeted retry on hop 2, and stop early once two grounded, consistent hops are achieved. Synthesize only the city span, not the intermediate organization.
entries.rationale: Winning trajectories enforced city-type answers, anchored hop 2 to canonical org sources (infobox/official site), avoided drift to ownership/off-type facts, cross-checked with parent listings, applied plausibility checks, and limited retries; losing ones output the bridge entity or wrong location and wasted tokens.

entries.text: Descriptor-propagating 3+ hop with title/work disambiguation and validation: Plan origin/context (country/domain) → uniquely qualified bridge entity (e.g., the ruler described by action Y) → terminal relation. First, expand and disambiguate any quoted/ambiguous work/title (normalize title/author/date/language) and anchor timeframe. Carry forward all descriptor constraints and the country/context across hops. Add a quick validation hop on the candidate bridge entity to confirm the specific qualifying property before proceeding. Keep retrieval narrowly scoped by era/country and stop once constraints are jointly satisfied; at synthesis, select only the terminal relation, not the bridge entity.
entries.rationale: Higher-scoring trajectories succeeded when they propagated the descriptive qualifier and disambiguated cited titles; lower-scoring ones dropped the qualifier and drifted to generic rulers. An explicit title/work disambiguation plus a validation hop reduces ambiguity and error propagation and is transferable to descriptor-anchored 3+ hop questions.

entries.text: Successor-government guardrail for 3+ hop chains: From a grounded, qualified ruler, enforce answer-type=government/regime on the final hop and require a chronology check so the answer is the immediate successor government in the same country/context. Reject individuals, dynasties, events, or processes as finals. Require a temporal anchor (ruler’s term end and regime start) or equivalent structured evidence; if uncertain, issue one targeted follow-up, otherwise stop early once a type-matching, time-consistent government is found. Synthesize only the government/regime name.
entries.rationale: Failures repeatedly returned off-type finals (events/dynasties/individuals) or wrong chronology. The better plans emphasized enforcing terminal type and temporal succession. This guardrail generalizes across successor/regime queries and prevents type/time drift at the last hop.

entries.text: Hidden-jurisdiction metric via affiliated entity (strict 2-hop): When a query routes a jurisdiction-scoped metric through a person/organization, plan a minimal bridge: (entity -> resolve governing jurisdiction/country/state -> fetch only the jurisdiction-level metric). Hop 1: disambiguate and ground the exact entity and its governing jurisdiction with high confidence (>=0.7), locking to the canonical page/ID; reuse the resolved jurisdiction string/ID verbatim in hop 2. Hop 2: issue one narrowly scoped lookup for the target metric constrained to the resolved jurisdiction and expected units/keywords; accept only if confidence >=0.7 with authoritative/structured evidence. Synthesis: enforce answer-type=numeric metric (unit-bearing when applicable); output only the terminal value, never the intermediate entity/jurisdiction. Efficiency: cap to one precise lookup per hop; do not retry already-confident hops; if hop 2 is weak, do a single targeted reformulation using the same jurisdiction plus specific metric+unit terms; stop early once both hops are grounded.
entries.rationale: Winning traces recognized the implicit bridge and separated jurisdiction resolution from metric retrieval, enforced confidence gates per hop, constrained the second hop with the resolved jurisdiction and expected units, and returned only the numeric terminal value. Losing traces collapsed to intermediates or produced off-type answers without units or sufficient grounding.

entries.text: Numeric/unit guardrail for policy/statistical answers: Derive the expected numeric type and units from the question (currency, rate, percentage, count, etc.). Accept only spans that include the number with the expected unit or a standardizable equivalent; normalize formats (e.g., symbol vs. code, percent sign vs. word) and enforce scope (jurisdiction/timeframe if specified). Prefer authoritative/structured sources and reject narrative mentions lacking units or clear scope. Block finalization if the span is off-type, missing units, or low-confidence (<0.7); re-query narrowly by adding the resolved jurisdiction/entity and exact metric keywords plus unit terms. Stop once a single high-confidence, unit-bearing value is found and synthesize only that terminal value.
entries.rationale: High-scoring runs consistently enforced numeric+unit validation and scoped retrieval to authoritative metric sources, while low-scoring ones emitted intermediates or unitless values. A reusable numeric guardrail improves precision across similar tasks.

entries.text: Superlative-to-entity numeric 2-hop with metric alignment: (1) Resolve the canonical entity implied by a superlative/descriptor with strict type/name/context checks and confidence ≥ 0.7; lock to the entity’s page/ID. (2) Determine which quantitative metric the superlative refers to (e.g., area vs volume vs capacity) and carry that metric and its synonyms forward. (3) Query only that metric on the locked entity, preferring structured/infobox fields or sentences adjacent to the attribute; require a digit-bearing value with a normalizable unit. Block finalization if the span lacks units or drifts to a different metric; if hop 2 is weak, do one targeted retry using metric-specific synonyms. Synthesis: enforce answer-type=numeric-with-units and output only the terminal value, never the bridge entity. Efficiency: one narrow lookup per hop, early stop once a high-confidence, type- and metric-aligned value is found; avoid extra hops or broad retries.
entries.rationale: Higher-scoring runs consistently used a tight 2-hop plan, first locking the referent of the superlative and then extracting a metric-aligned numeric with units from structured or nearby text. Lower-scoring runs over-expanded to extra hops and failed to align the metric or enforce numeric+unit validation, leading to off-type finals.

entries.text: Numeric guardrail for entity attributes: derive the expected numeric type and likely unit from the question and context (e.g., floor area/capacity/height). Accept only spans with explicit digits plus a standardizable unit; normalize unit labels and formats and prefer explicit numerals over narrative phrases. Reject entity names or unitless values at synthesis and gate acceptance on confidence ≥ 0.7. If low confidence or missing units, issue a single targeted re-query using attribute-specific synonyms (e.g., gross/total floor area, square feet/meters) and prefer structured sources/infoboxes or sentences closest to the attribute field. Keep retrieval tight (top-k sentences near the attribute) and stop early once a validated, unit-bearing value is found.
entries.rationale: Winning trajectories enforced a strict numeric-with-units final type, applied synonym-driven retries only when needed, and kept retrieval near structured fields; the weak run lacked these guardrails and drifted to entities or off-type spans.

entries.text: Type/descriptor-guarded, efficient 2-hop with direct-lookup fallback: derive the expected answer type and carry forward key descriptors and regional cues to constrain hop-1; require high-confidence grounding and lock hop 2 to the resolved entity’s exact page/ID. Issue one narrowly scoped lookup per hop, reuse hop-1’s ID/title directly for hop 2, and constrain hop 2 to the known region. Query only the requested terminal attribute and enforce an answer-type guard; reject off-type or related-but-different properties. Prefer canonical/authoritative sources and perform a lightweight cross-check. If hop-2 fails type/region checks or is low-confidence, auto-refine and retry minimal alternates for hop 1 once; when hop 1 remains uncertain, attempt one scoped 1-hop direct lookup of the target attribute as a sanity check. Avoid redundant retries/parallel branches and stop early once two grounded, consistent hops are achieved. Synthesize only the terminal attribute, not the bridge entity.
